title: Taking Advantage of 2-0
description: You're batting with a 2-0 count in a tie game. The pitcher just missed with two curveballs. On 2-0 counts, batters hit .350 or better because pitchers usually have to throw strikes.
options: Look for a pitch in your favorite hitting zone and swing hard if you get it
Swing at anything close to avoid falling behind in the count
Take the next pitch no matter what to try for 3-0
Choke up and just try to make contact
explanations: Looking for your pitch is perfect! On 2-0, pitchers need to throw strikes, so you can wait for a good pitch to hit. This is why batters hit so well on 2-0 counts.
Swinging at anything gives away your advantage. You're ahead 2-0, not behind! The pitcher is the one who needs to throw strikes.
Taking no matter what wastes a chance to hit a good pitch. While 3-0 is nice, you might miss a perfect pitch to hit on 2-0.
Just trying to make contact wastes your power advantage. On 2-0, you should be looking to hit the ball hard, not just put it in play.
concept: Understanding that 2-0 is a hitter's count where you can be selective and aggressive

title: Catching the Borderline Pitch
description: Top 5, runner on second, and you're behind the plate. Your pitcher delivers a 2-2 fastball that catches the very edge of the zone. The umpire hesitates — this pitch could go either way.
options: Hold your glove perfectly still where you caught it and keep your body quiet
Subtly pull your glove an inch or two toward the center while keeping your body still
Reach out and catch the ball early before it crosses the plate
Move your glove dramatically to where the pitch should have been
explanations: Excellent! By holding your glove still and keeping your body quiet, you show the umpire exactly where the pitch crossed the plate. Good catchers know that stillness helps umpires make accurate calls.
Moving your glove after catching makes it look like the pitch wasn't really a strike. This actually hurts your pitcher's chances of getting the call.
Catching the ball early makes it impossible for the umpire to see where it crossed the plate. Always let the ball travel into your glove.
Dramatic glove movement tells everyone you think it was a ball. You're making the umpire's job harder instead of easier.
concept: Good catchers help umpires by holding still and showing where the pitch crossed

title: Reading the Line Drive
description: You're playing center field with runners on second and third, one out. The batter rips a line drive to right-center that's slicing away from you. You need to pick the best route to make this catch.
options: Take an angle route, running diagonally back and to your right
Sprint straight back without reading the ball's trajectory
Run directly at where the ball appears to be going now
Wait a moment to read it better, then sprint to the spot
explanations: Taking an angle route lets you maintain speed while adjusting to the ball's slice. Your efficient first step and diagonal path give you the best chance to reach this tough liner.
Sprinting straight back ignores the ball's rightward movement. You'd have to stop and change direction, losing all your momentum when the ball slices away.
Running directly at the ball's current position doesn't account for its slice. You'll end up chasing it sideways and probably come up short.
Waiting to read the ball wastes precious time. With outfielder first-step efficiency improving catch probability by 23%, you need to commit to your route immediately.
concept: Efficient angle routes on slicing line drives maximize your range and catch probability

title: First Pitch Strategy
description: Top of the 5th, your team trails 2-1. Their cleanup hitter steps up to lead off the inning with bases empty. You need to start this at-bat right.
options: Throw a get-me-over fastball down the middle
Start with your best breaking ball to get ahead
Attack the edge of the zone with your fastball
Throw a ball on purpose to set up your strikeout pitch
explanations: A get-me-over fastball is too predictable - good hitters feast on predictable first pitches. Against their cleanup hitter, you can't afford to groove one.
Your breaking ball might surprise them, but if you miss the zone, you're behind 1-0 where hitters have a significant advantage. First pitch strikes are too important to gamble.
Attacking the edge gives you the best of both worlds - you can get a strike without giving them a pitch to drive. Getting to 0-2 dramatically shifts the at-bat in your favor.
Starting with a ball puts you behind immediately. In a 1-0 count, hitters have the advantage. You need to work ahead, not behind.
concept: First pitch strikes are critical - get ahead without giving hitters something to drive

title: Two-Strike Battle with Runner in Scoring Position
description: Top 5, you're at the plate with a 2-2 count and your teammate on second base. Your team trails 2-1, and this runner represents the tying run. The pitcher has been tough today, and you need to adjust your approach.
options: Choke up on the bat and protect the plate
Keep your normal swing and look for a pitch to drive
Take the next pitch hoping for ball three
Try a surprise bunt to advance the runner
explanations: With two strikes, you need to shorten your swing and protect the plate. Choking up gives you better bat control to foul off tough pitches and make contact. Your batting average may drop from .260 to .190 with two strikes, but making contact keeps the inning alive and can advance your runner.

Keeping your normal swing with two strikes is risky. While you might drive the ball if you connect, the expanded strike zone means you'll likely swing through pitches on the edges. Two-strike batting averages drop significantly because full swings can't cover the whole zone.

Taking a pitch with a 2-2 count is dangerous. The pitcher knows you're behind and will attack the zone. With two strikes, umpires often expand the strike zone slightly, so taking a borderline pitch could end your at-bat without helping your team.

Bunting with two strikes is extremely risky. If you foul it off, you're out automatically. Even successful bunts rarely advance runners from second to third with one out, and you'd be giving up a chance to drive in the tying run.
concept: With two strikes, shorten your swing and protect the plate to keep the at-bat alive

title: Shift Against Pull Hitter
description: You're playing third base in the top of the 5th. Your team trails 3-2 with a runner on first and one out. The batter is a right-handed power hitter who pulls 75% of his ground balls to your side.
options: Shift toward shortstop for better double play positioning
Play deep and guard the line to prevent extra bases
Move in closer for a possible bunt or slow roller
Stay in standard position and react to whatever happens
explanations: With the runner on first and this pull hitter's tendencies, you should shift toward the shortstop hole. This gives you the best chance to turn two on a ground ball, which could end the inning and preserve your one-run deficit.
Playing deep to guard the line prevents doubles, but with a runner already on first and trailing by one, you need to focus on getting outs rather than preventing extra bases.
Moving in for a bunt is too aggressive here. This power hitter rarely bunts, and you'd be out of position for his typical hard ground balls to your area.
Staying in standard position ignores the scouting data. When you know 75% of his ground balls come to the left side, you need to adjust your positioning to take advantage of that tendency.
concept: Defensive shifts maximize your chances of making plays based on hitter tendencies

title: Steal Threat With Full Count
description: You're catching in the bottom of the 5th, down 3-2. There's a speedy runner on first with one out and a full count on the batter. You know this runner loves to steal on 3-2 counts.
options: Stay low in your normal crouch to avoid tipping that you expect a steal
Shift your weight slightly forward onto your toes for a quicker release
Move up closer to the plate to shorten your throw distance to second
Call for a pitchout to get a clean throw if the runner goes
explanations: Staying in your normal crouch might keep the element of surprise, but you'll be slower getting into throwing position. Elite catchers need 1.9-2.0 second pop times, and starting flat-footed costs you precious time.

Shifting your weight forward onto your toes lets you explode into your throw while still receiving the pitch normally. This pre-loading shaves 0.1-0.2 seconds off your pop time without tipping your hand to the runner.

Moving closer to the plate seems logical to shorten the distance, but you'll likely get called for catcher's interference if the batter swings. Plus, you still need proper footwork for an accurate throw.
A pitchout on 3-2 gives the batter a free base with a walk. With your team already trailing, you can't afford to put another runner on base voluntarily.
concept: Catchers can improve pop time by pre-loading weight forward while maintaining normal receiving position

title: Double Play Pivot - Set Your Feet
description: Top 5, your team leads 3-2. Runner on first, nobody out. Sharp grounder to your second baseman who flips to you at second base. You catch the ball as you approach the bag.
options: Plant your right foot on the back corner of the bag, catch, pivot, and throw
Catch the ball and immediately throw without setting your feet
Stay on the bag after catching to ensure the force out
Jump straight up to avoid the runner while throwing
explanations: Planting your right foot on the back corner gives you the best balance and throwing angle. You create momentum toward first while clearing the baseline - this footwork increases double play success by 35%.
Throwing without setting your feet leads to wild throws. You might beat the runner but an errant throw gives them both bases.
Staying on the bag after the catch leaves you vulnerable to the sliding runner and makes your throw awkward. You already have the force - now you need the double play.
Jumping straight up takes away all your throwing power and accuracy. You'll float the ball to first and the runner will beat it easily.
concept: Proper pivot footwork on double plays - right foot on back corner, catch, pivot, throw

title: Reading the Hit-and-Run Signal
description: Bottom 5th, game tied 2-2. You're on first base with 1 out. Your manager gives you the hit-and-run sign — you must go on the pitcher's first move to help the batter avoid a double play.
options: Break for second as soon as the pitcher starts his motion toward home
Wait to see if the batter makes contact before running
Hold until the ball crosses the plate, then decide
Take a big lead but only go if you see a good pitch to hit
explanations: On the hit-and-run, you must break immediately when the pitcher commits to home. This forces the defense to cover second base, creating holes for the batter. Your early jump also prevents double plays on ground balls.
Waiting for contact defeats the purpose of the hit-and-run. If you wait, the defense won't have to cover second base early, and you'll be an easy double play victim on any ground ball.
Waiting until the ball crosses the plate is way too late. You'd have no chance to reach second on a ground ball, resulting in an easy double play that kills your rally.
The hit-and-run requires total commitment — you go regardless of the pitch location. Taking a big lead without running just makes you vulnerable to a pickoff.
concept: Hit-and-run requires the runner to break on the pitcher's first move, creating defensive holes and preventing double plays

title: Cutoff or Go Home Direct
description: You're playing right field in a tight game. With your team leading 3-2 in the Top 5th, there's a runner on second with one out. The batter lines a single to right that you field cleanly on one hop.
options: Hit your cutoff man (first baseman) with a strong throw
Throw directly home to try preventing the run
Let the ball go through to the wall for a faster relay
Throw to second base to keep the batter from advancing
explanations: Hitting your cutoff gives you the best chance to prevent the run. Your throw to the cutoff has an 85% success rate versus only 50% throwing directly home. The cutoff can redirect if the runner stops or make a shorter, more accurate throw home.

While throwing directly home seems aggressive, your 50% success rate from right field means you're more likely to overthrow and allow extra bases. Even with your strong arm, accuracy drops significantly at this distance.
Letting the ball go through wastes precious time and eliminates any chance of getting the runner. By the time you retrieve it from the wall, the run will score easily and the batter might take third.
Throwing to second ignores the immediate threat of the runner scoring from second. You'd be conceding the run to maybe prevent one extra base - a terrible trade when you're only up by one run.
concept: Hit the cutoff man for better accuracy and options on throws from the outfield

title: Positioning with Runners in Scoring Position
description: You're the third baseman. Bottom of the 6th, your team trails 4-3. Runners on second and third with one out. The batter is 2-for-3 today with solid contact.
options: Move in halfway to cut off the run at home on any ground ball
Play deep to prevent an extra base hit down the line
Guard the line to prevent a double that could score both runners
Stay at normal depth but shade toward the shortstop hole
explanations: Moving in halfway gives you the best chance to throw home on a ground ball. With 1.44 expected runs in this situation, preventing the lead runner from scoring is crucial. You can still make plays on hard-hit balls from this position.
Playing deep might prevent an extra base hit, but you give up any chance of throwing home on a grounder. The runner on third will score easily, and you're already behind by one run.
Guarding the line prevents a double, but with runners already in scoring position, your priority is preventing runs, not extra bases. A single up the middle scores both runners anyway.
Normal depth leaves you too far back to make a play at home. In a one-run game, you need to be aggressive and give your team a chance to get the lead runner.
concept: With runners in scoring position in a close game, third basemen must balance preventing runs versus preventing extra base hits

title: Backup Positioning on Right Field Hit
description: You're the left fielder in the top of the 5th. Your team leads 3-2 with a runner on second and no outs. The batter hits a deep fly ball to right field that might score the runner.
options: Sprint to back up third base in case of an overthrow
Stay in your normal left field position to cover your area
Run toward home plate to back up the catcher
Move to shallow center field to help cover the infield
explanations: Backing up third base is your primary responsibility here. With the runner trying to score from second, any overthrow from right field or a relay throw could get past third base, and you need to be there to prevent extra bases.
Staying in left field leaves third base unprotected. If there's an overthrow or the runner holds at third, you won't be in position to help.
The catcher already has the pitcher backing up home plate. Your job as the left fielder is to back up third base on balls hit to the right side.
Moving to shallow center doesn't help anyone. The middle infielders will handle the relay, and you need to be backing up third base, not floating in no-man's land.
concept: Left fielders back up third base on balls hit to the right side of the field

title: Tag Up or Stay?
description: You're on second base with nobody out. The batter hits a high fly ball to center field. Do you tag up and try for third?
options: Tag up and go to third after the catch
Run right away when the ball is hit
Wait to see if it drops
Stay at second base
explanations: You tag up and advance! By waiting for the catch, you follow the rules and still get to third base safely.
You can't run right away on a fly ball. You must wait for the catch or you'll be out.
You can't wait to see - you must go back to second base until the ball is caught.
You miss a chance to advance. With nobody out, getting to third base is important.
concept: On fly balls, baserunners must touch their base after the catch before advancing

title: Double Play Pivot at Second
description: You're playing shortstop in the top of the 4th. Your team leads 2-1. The second baseman fields a grounder and tosses it to you for the double play.
options: Catch the ball, step on the bag with your right foot, pivot, and throw to first
Catch the ball and throw to first right away without touching the bag
Wait for the runner to get really close, then make your pivot
Jump over the bag while catching and throwing at the same time
explanations: You do it just right! You catch the ball, touch the bag with your right foot to get the force out, then pivot and throw to first. Using your right foot keeps your body open to first base and helps you avoid the sliding runner while making a strong throw.
You forgot to touch second base! Without stepping on the bag, you don't get the force out at second. Now you only get one out instead of two.
You wait too long! The runner slides into you before you can make your throw. You need to be quick but also use the right footwork.
You try to do too much at once! Jumping makes your throw wild. You need to use proper footwork - catch, step, pivot, throw.
concept: Double play pivot footwork protects you from sliding runners

title: Platoon Pinch-Hit Decision
description: Top of the 6th, you're managing and trailing 3-2. Your left-handed batter is due up with a runner on first and one out. The opposing manager just brought in their tough left-handed reliever.
options: Pinch-hit with your right-handed bench player who hits .290 vs lefties
Keep your left-handed starter in - he's 2-for-3 today
Wait until there are 2 outs to make a move
Pinch-hit with your best overall hitter who happens to be left-handed
explanations: Right-handed batters usually hit much better against left-handed pitchers than lefties do. Your righty hits .290 against lefties - that's the matchup advantage you want to use here!
Being hot today doesn't change the fact that left-handed batters typically struggle against left-handed pitchers. Same-side matchups favor the pitcher, not the hitter.
Waiting wastes an at-bat where you could use the righty-lefty advantage. With a runner already on, you need to maximize every opportunity to score in this close game.
Even your best hitter faces a big disadvantage batting left-handed against a lefty pitcher. The righty-lefty matchup matters more than overall skill here.
concept: Right-handed batters have significant advantages against left-handed pitchers compared to lefties

title: Rundown Between Third and Home
description: You're playing third base in a close game. Your team leads 3-2 in the bottom of the 5th. With runners on second and third, the runner from third gets caught in a rundown between you and the catcher.
options: Set up properly between third and home, forcing the runner back toward third while watching the trail runner
Chase the runner all the way to home plate yourself to make the tag
Immediately throw to the catcher without trying to get the runner moving back toward third
Leave the rundown to handle the trail runner at second base instead
explanations: This is the correct rundown technique. You want to force the runner back toward third (where he came from) while keeping an eye on the trail runner at second. Stay in the baseline, pump fake if needed, and make crisp throws to minimize the number of tosses.
Chasing the runner yourself takes too long and lets the trail runner advance to third easily. In a rundown, you want to work together with your teammates using quick throws, not long chases.
Throwing immediately to home doesn't establish the rundown properly. You need to get the runner moving back toward third first, making it harder for him to safely return to the base.
Abandoning the lead runner in a rundown almost guarantees he scores. Your shortstop should be covering third base while you handle the rundown with the catcher.
concept: In a rundown, force the runner back toward the previous base while minimizing throws

title: Double Play Depth Decision
description: You're the third baseman with a runner on first and no outs. The away team leads 3-2 in the fifth inning. Your team needs a double play to get out of this jam without giving up more runs.
options: Position yourself at double play depth, a few steps in and toward second base
Play deep near the line to protect against an extra base hit
Shade toward the shortstop to help with up-the-middle coverage
Play normal depth but cheat a step toward first base
explanations: You position yourself at double play depth for the 5-4-3. This reduces your reaction time by 0.3 seconds but increases your chance of turning two by 35%. With no outs and the tying run on base, getting two outs is worth the trade-off.
Playing deep protects against doubles but makes it nearly impossible for you to start a 5-4-3 double play. You'd need a perfect throw just to get one out at second.
Shading toward shortstop leaves the line vulnerable and doesn't help much with double plays since you still need to cover third base. The shortstop already has the middle covered.
Normal depth with a step toward first doesn't give you the angle you need for a quick throw to second. You need to be closer and more toward second base for an effective 5-4-3.
concept: Double play depth positioning increases 5-4-3 conversion rates

title: Double Play Pivot — Avoiding the Slide
description: Bot 5, no outs, your team trails 3-2. Runner on first takes off with the pitch. The batter hits a sharp grounder to second base. Your second baseman fields it cleanly and flips to you covering the bag.
options: Catch the ball, touch the bag, then jump to your right while throwing
Stay planted on the bag and make a strong throw
Jump straight up to avoid the runner
Catch and immediately leap backwards while throwing
explanations: This is the proper pivot technique — you secure the out at second, then use your momentum to jump toward first base while throwing. This gets you clear of the sliding runner while maintaining accuracy on your throw.
Staying planted on the bag puts you directly in the path of the sliding runner. You'll likely get taken out before completing your throw, and the contact could cause an errant throw or injury.
Jumping straight up leaves you hanging in the air with no balance or momentum for your throw. You'll have no power behind the throw and poor accuracy while airborne.
Leaping backwards takes you away from first base, making your throw longer and weaker. You also risk landing awkwardly or losing sight of first base.
concept: On double play pivots, jump toward first base while throwing to avoid the sliding runner and maintain throwing accuracy

title: Pitcher Covering First on Grounder
description: You're pitching in the bottom of the 3rd with your team up 2-1. The batter hits a slow grounder to the right side. Your first baseman fields it but he's too far from the bag to beat the runner.
options: Sprint to first base to take the throw from your first baseman
Stay on the mound and let the first baseman handle it alone
Run toward the ball to help field it
Cover home plate in case there's a play there
explanations: You sprint to first base and take the toss from your first baseman for the out. Whenever the first baseman fields a ball away from the bag, it's the pitcher's job to cover first. You run along the inside of the baseline to avoid the runner.
Staying on the mound means nobody covers first base. The first baseman can't field the ball AND beat the runner to the bag. You just gave the other team a free baserunner.
Running toward the ball creates confusion. Your first baseman already has it fielded. You need to be at the bag, not at the ball. Two fielders going for the same ball leaves the base uncovered.
There's no play at home with nobody on base. Covering home makes no sense here. Your job is to cover first base whenever the first baseman is pulled off the bag.
concept: Pitchers must cover first base when the first baseman fields a ball away from the bag

title: First Baseman Holding the Runner
description: You're playing first base in the top of the 4th. Your team leads 3-1 with a speedy runner on first and one out. The runner has already stolen two bases today. Your pitcher is in the stretch.
options: Stay close to the bag with your foot on it to keep the runner honest
Play behind the runner at normal depth to cover more ground
Cheat toward second base to help with a potential steal throw
Stand right next to the runner to intimidate him into staying close
explanations: Staying close to the bag with your foot on it keeps the runner from taking a big lead. This makes it harder for him to steal because your pitcher can throw over quickly. Once the pitch is delivered, you release and get into fielding position.
Playing behind the runner gives him a huge lead. With his speed, he'll steal easily because there's no one holding him close to the bag. You're giving him a free head start.
Cheating toward second base leaves first base completely uncovered. If your pitcher tries to pick off, nobody is there to catch it. You also can't field anything hit to the right side.

Standing next to the runner without your foot on the bag doesn't actually keep him close. You need to be on the bag so your pitcher can throw over for a pickoff attempt. Just standing there is wasted effort.
concept: First basemen hold runners by keeping a foot on the bag, allowing quick pickoff throws

title: Center Fielder Communication on Shallow Fly
description: You're playing center field in the bottom of the 5th. The batter pops up a fly ball into shallow center. Your shortstop is running back for it and you're charging in. You can see the ball clearly.
options: Call 'I got it!' loud and clear, wave off the shortstop
Stay quiet and let the shortstop take it since he's closer
Both go for it — whoever gets there first catches it
Pull up and point at the shortstop to let him know it's his
explanations: You call 'I got it!' loud and clear. As the center fielder, you have priority over all infielders on fly balls because you're running forward with better momentum and a clearer view. When you call it, the shortstop peels off and avoids a collision.
Staying quiet is dangerous. Without communication, you and the shortstop could collide at full speed. The center fielder has priority and must take charge by calling for the ball.
Both going for it without talking is how collisions happen. One player must call for the ball, and in center field, that's you. You have priority over infielders on fly balls.

Pulling up and pointing wastes time and might confuse the shortstop. The shortstop is running with his back to the infield and may not see your hand signal. Use your voice — it's the only reliable way to communicate.
concept: Center fielders have priority over infielders on fly balls and must communicate loudly

title: Batter Adjusting to Off-Speed After Fastballs
description: You're batting in the bottom of the 6th with your team trailing 4-3. Runner on second, one out. The pitcher has thrown you three straight fastballs this at-bat. On a 2-1 count, you notice the pitcher's arm speed looks different as he releases the next pitch.
options: Recognize the slower arm speed and wait back on the changeup
Swing hard expecting another fastball — he's been throwing heat all at-bat
Take the pitch no matter what since it might be a ball
Start your swing early to make sure you get around on it
explanations: You read the pitcher's arm speed and recognize the off-speed pitch. By staying back and keeping your weight on your back foot a little longer, you can drive the changeup to the opposite field and bring home the tying run from second.

Swinging hard expecting a fastball when the arm speed is slower means you'll be way out in front. You'll either miss completely or hit a weak ground ball. Good hitters adjust to what they see, not what they expect.
Taking this pitch is too passive. You're behind in the game and have a hitter's count at 2-1. With a runner in scoring position, you need to be aggressive on a hittable pitch.
Starting your swing early is the opposite of what you should do on an off-speed pitch. You need to wait longer, not swing sooner. Early swings on changeups produce weak contact or swings and misses.
concept: Recognizing off-speed pitches by reading arm speed helps batters adjust their timing

title: Baserunner Reading a Ball in the Dirt
description: You're the runner on third base in the bottom of the 7th, game tied 3-3 with two outs. The pitcher throws a curveball that bounces in the dirt in front of the catcher. The catcher knocks it down but the ball rolls a few feet away.
options: Read the ball off the catcher — it rolled far enough, sprint home for the go-ahead run
Stay at third — too risky with two outs to get thrown out
Take a big secondary lead but don't commit to going home
Break for home as soon as the ball hits the dirt, before reading the bounce
explanations: You read the ball off the catcher perfectly. The ball rolled far enough that the catcher has to turn and chase it, giving you time to score. With two outs in a tied game, this aggressive baserunning wins the game.

Playing it safe here costs your team a chance to take the lead. The ball rolled several feet away — the catcher can't make a play on you. Great baserunners read the situation and take what the defense gives them.
A big secondary lead without committing wastes your opportunity. By the time you decide to go, the catcher will have recovered the ball. You need to read and react in one motion.

Breaking before reading the bounce is reckless. What if the catcher blocks it cleanly and the ball stays right in front of him? You'd be out by ten feet. Always read the ball off the catcher first, then go.
concept: Baserunners on third should read balls in the dirt off the catcher before committing to scoring

title: Manager Bringing in the Closer
description: You're managing in the top of the 9th, leading 5-4. Your starter has thrown 95 pitches and just gave up a double. Runner on second, nobody out. Your closer is warmed up and your starter looks tired.
options: Pull your starter and bring in the closer to protect the lead
Leave your starter in — he got you this far, let him finish
Bring in a setup man first, save the closer for a save situation with the lead
Have your starter face one more batter to see if he settles down
explanations: You bring in the closer right away. With the tying run on second and nobody out in the 9th, this is the highest-leverage moment of the game. Your starter is tired at 95 pitches and just gave up a double — every sign says it's time for your best reliever.

Loyalty to your starter can lose you games. He's showing clear signs of fatigue — 95 pitches and a double. The 9th inning with a one-run lead is exactly when you need your closer, not your tired starter.

Saving the closer for later doesn't make sense when the game is on the line right now. If the tying run scores, there's no save situation to protect. Use your best pitcher in the most important moment.
Facing one more batter is gambling with your lead. If your tired starter gives up another hit, the game is tied and you're in trouble. The risk of waiting far outweighs any benefit.
concept: Managers should bring in their closer in the highest-leverage moment, not save them for a traditional save situation

title: Rules: Designated Hitter Confusion
description: You're watching a game where the designated hitter (DH) has been batting for the pitcher all game. In the 6th inning, the manager wants to move the DH to play first base and bring in a new pitcher. What happens to the DH?
options: The DH is lost — the new pitcher must bat in the lineup
The DH can play first base and still be the DH
The DH can play first base, and a new DH can be designated for the new pitcher
This move is illegal — the DH can never play a defensive position
explanations: When a DH enters the game as a fielder, the DH role is terminated for the rest of the game. The new pitcher must take the DH's spot in the batting order, and from now on the pitcher has to bat.
Once the DH plays defense, he's no longer the DH. He becomes a regular position player. You can't be both a fielder and a DH at the same time in the same game.
You can't create a new DH mid-game. The DH is set at the start. Once the original DH plays a position, the DH spot is gone for good. The pitcher must now bat.
The DH can move to a defensive position — it's not illegal. But doing so means you lose the DH for the rest of the game. Managers need to weigh whether the defensive upgrade is worth losing the DH.
concept: When the DH enters the game as a fielder, the DH role is lost and the pitcher must bat

title: Second Baseman Turning Two on a Grounder to Short
description: You're playing second base in the top of the 3rd. Runner on first, no outs, your team leads 1-0. The batter hits a sharp grounder to the shortstop, who fields it cleanly and fires to you at second base.
options: Catch the ball, touch the bag, and quickly step toward first to make your throw
Catch the ball while running across the bag and throw on the run
Stand on the bag, catch, and make a strong throw from a standstill
Catch the ball behind the bag and reach back to tag it before throwing
explanations: You catch, touch the bag for the force out, then step toward first base to generate momentum for an accurate throw. This is the textbook double play turn — it gets you clear of the runner while maintaining throwing accuracy.
Running across the bag while catching and throwing often leads to wild throws. Without setting your feet, you lose accuracy and arm strength. Speed matters, but not at the cost of a bad throw.
Standing still on the bag makes you a sitting duck for the sliding runner. You need to get off the bag quickly after the force out. Standing there also reduces your throwing velocity.
Catching behind the bag and reaching back wastes time and creates an awkward throwing angle. You want momentum going toward first, not reaching backward toward second.
concept: On double play turns, the second baseman touches the bag then steps toward first for an accurate relay throw

title: Famous: Jackie Robinson Stealing Home
description: It's the 1955 World Series, Game 1. You're Jackie Robinson on third base. The Yankees lead 6-4 in the bottom of the 8th. Whitey Ford is pitching from the full windup with a left-handed batter at the plate.
options: Stay at third — stealing home is too risky this late in the game
Wait for a passed ball or wild pitch to advance
Break for home on Ford's windup — his slow delivery gives you a window
Bluff toward home to distract the pitcher and help the batter
explanations: Playing it safe doesn't fit this moment. Down two runs in the 8th inning of the World Series, you need to create pressure. Jackie Robinson was famous for his daring baserunning that rattled opposing pitchers.
Waiting for a wild pitch is passive. You can't count on the pitcher making a mistake. Elite baserunners like Jackie Robinson created their own opportunities by studying the pitcher's mechanics.

You time Ford's slow windup and break for home! His full windup takes longer to deliver the ball, giving you just enough time to slide in ahead of Yogi Berra's tag. This steal of home electrified Ebbets Field and showed that aggressive baserunning can change a game's momentum.

A bluff might distract Ford, but it doesn't score a run. With your team down two in the 8th, you need runs on the board, not distractions. Jackie Robinson's boldness was about action, not just threats.
concept: Stealing home is possible against pitchers with slow windups when the element of surprise is on your side

title: Left Fielder Playing the Sun
description: You're playing left field in the bottom of the 4th on a sunny afternoon. Your team leads 2-1. The batter lifts a high fly ball toward you, but the sun is directly in your eyes. You lose the ball in the glare.
options: Use your glove to shade the sun and pick up the ball's trajectory
Close your eyes and guess where the ball will land
Turn your back to the ball and run to where you think it's going
Call off the play and let the center fielder take it
explanations: You use your glove as a sun shield, holding it up to block the glare while tracking the ball with your other eye. This is the technique every outfielder learns for day games. You find the ball, adjust, and make the catch.
Closing your eyes and guessing is never the answer. You'll misjudge the ball completely and it'll drop for extra bases. There's always a way to find the ball — use your glove as a sun visor.
Turning your back means you lose all track of the ball. You're running blind and hoping for the best. Keep facing the ball and use your glove to shield the sun.

Calling off the play when you're the closest fielder doesn't make sense unless the center fielder has a better angle on the ball. Your first responsibility is to try to make the play using proper sun-field technique.
concept: Outfielders use their glove to shade the sun while tracking fly balls on sunny days

title: Right Fielder Deciding on a Dive
description: You're playing right field in the top of the 7th. Your team leads 3-2 with runners on first and second, one out. The batter lines a sinking shot toward the right-center gap. You're sprinting but it's going to be close.
options: Play it on a hop to keep the ball in front of you and limit the damage
Dive for the catch — if you snag it, runners can't advance
Let the center fielder take it since he might have a better angle
Sprint past the ball and try to play it off the wall
explanations: You play it on a hop to keep the ball in front of you. With runners on first and second, a diving attempt that misses rolls all the way to the wall and both runners score. Playing it safe holds the runners and keeps it a one-run game.

Diving is risky here. If you miss, the ball gets past you and rolls to the wall. Both runners score easily, and the batter might get a triple. With a one-run lead, you can't afford to let both runners score.
Calling off the center fielder when you're closer wastes time. You're the one in the best position to field this ball. Take charge and play it on a hop.
Sprinting past the ball makes no sense. You'll overrun it and it'll be behind you. Play the ball in front of you, not behind you.
concept: With runners on base and a close lead, outfielders should play sinking liners on a hop to prevent extra bases

title: Counting the Count: Working a Walk
description: You're batting in the bottom of the 5th with your team trailing 3-2. Nobody on base, two outs. The pitcher has thrown three balls and one strike. On 3-1, the next pitch looks like it might be just off the outside corner.
options: Take the pitch — with a 3-1 count, make the pitcher prove he can throw a strike
Swing at it — you might not get a better pitch to hit
Bunt to try to surprise the defense
Swing as hard as you can — you need to drive in runs
explanations: On 3-1, the advantage is yours. If the pitch is borderline, take it. The pitcher needs to throw a strike or you walk. Getting on base with two outs keeps the inning alive and puts pressure on the pitcher. A walk is as good as a hit here.
Swinging at a borderline 3-1 pitch bails the pitcher out. He's been struggling to throw strikes. If you swing and miss or hit a weak grounder, you just ended the inning when you could have walked.
Bunting on 3-1 with two outs and nobody on makes no sense. You're one pitch away from a walk. A bunt is almost always an out, and there's nobody on base to advance.
Swinging as hard as you can at a borderline pitch is undisciplined. The count favors you. Be patient, and if it's not a clear strike, take your base. Discipline at the plate wins games.
concept: On a 3-1 count, batters have the advantage and should be selective — take borderline pitches
